html:
The Sign of the Four — 10 Most Likely AQA Questions + Skeleton Essay Plans

For: AQA GCSE English Literature, Paper 1 Section B (19th-century novel). 30 marks. ~50 minutes.

Source. The plans below distil the ideas, frames and analytical moves from Sophicly's 9 Grade-9 Sign of the Four model answers (`Model Answers/AQA/Sign of the Four/`). Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes, the same controlling concept, and the same reading-strategies students have already learned in the Sign of the Four crib sheet.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

1. The AQA-style question.

2. The full typical extract in a blockquote so the student knows what scene to expect.

3. The frame line (the controlling argument behind the plan).

4. A short technique-term gloss block so every term in the plan is translated into plain English.

5. A skeleton plan in the locked structure used across every Sophicly worked essay:

• Introduction — 3 sentences (~110 words): Hook + Building sentence + Thesis.

• Body Paragraph 1 — 7 sentences (Beginning of the novel; ~210–225 words).

• Body Paragraph 2 — 7 sentences (Middle of the novel; ~225–250 words).

• Body Paragraph 3 — 7 sentences (End of the novel; ~225–250 words).

• Conclusion — 4 sentences (~110–155 words).

Total ~1000 words on the page in the exam.

The seven-sentence body-paragraph shape (memorise this):

1. Topic — pure concept, no techniques named yet.

2. Technique + evidence — name the technique and quote the line.

3. Close analysis — the words inside the quote, unpacked.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader feels or thinks.

5. Effect on reader 2 — a different feeling or thought.

6. Author's purpose — what Conan Doyle may be arguing.

7. Context (AO3) — one factor, woven in, never bolted on.

Whole-essay cohesion (Sophicly standard). Every step of the plan must form a single logical chain. BP2's topic sentence opens with "Where Chapter X had installed Y, Chapter Z extends/escalates/converts that into…"; BP3's topic sentence opens with "Where Chapter Z had Y, Chapter W names/destroys/redeems it into…"; the conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns. The three BPs operate at three different LEVELS of the same diagnosis.

Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes — the Sign of the Four corpus rests on Chapter 1 (the cocaine threshold, the watch deduction), Chapter 2 (Mary Morstan's arrival at Baker Street), Chapter 3 (the cab journey across Lambeth), Chapter 4 (Thaddeus Sholto's apartment), Chapter 5 (Pondicherry Lodge, Bartholomew's body in moonlight), Chapter 6 (Athelney Jones at the crime scene), Chapter 9–10 (the Thames boat chase, Tonga's death), Chapter 11 (the empty treasure-box, the proposal), and Chapter 12 (Jonathan Small's confession of Andaman, treasure, and betrayal). Memorise these scenes once, with two embedded quotations per scene, and every AQA question becomes a recombination of the same corpus.

---

Memorise these once

Controlling concept of The Sign of the Four. The British Empire produces the wealth, the violence and the crime that the British detective is then called upon to explain — Holmes's deductive method dramatises the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away, while the novel's trajectory (Watson's Afghan wound, the Agra treasure, Tonga's death, Small's confession) keeps showing the empire returning home as the very mystery the detective is solving.

Six key terms that recur in the plans below — gloss every one when you use it.

• Detective fiction = the genre Edgar Allan Poe founded with "The Murders in the Rue Morgue" (1841) — a brilliant rational detective, a slower narrator-companion, a locked-room or cryptic puzzle. The Sign of the Four (1890) is the second Holmes novel, written deliberately inside that Poe-inherited form.

• Deductive method = reasoning from observed details to a hidden conclusion. Conan Doyle modelled Holmes on Dr Joseph Bell, the Edinburgh medical professor whose diagnostic showmanship — naming a patient's occupation and recent travel from posture and clothing alone — Conan Doyle had watched as a student.

• Frame narrative = a story told inside another story's outer container. Watson narrates the case, but Jonathan Small's confession (Chapter 12) is a long second narrative recessed inside the first — the empire's story the detective story has been wrapped around.

• Imperial Gothic = late-Victorian narrative form in which the empire returns home as horror. Patrick Brantlinger named the genre in Rule of Darkness (1988); The Sign of the Four is one of its founding texts.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Watson as foil to Holmes; Athelney Jones as foil to Holmes; Small as foil to Watson).

• Free indirect discourse = a narrative voice that slides between the narrator and the character's interior; Conan Doyle uses it sparingly inside Watson's first-person but lets Small's confession speak in Small's own voice for the whole of Chapter 12.

Always gloss the term in brackets the first time you use it in an essay. AQA examiners reward the term + the gloss together.

---

Q1 — The character of Sherlock Holmes

Preamble

In this extract, Watson watches Holmes injecting a seven-per-cent solution of cocaine and challenges him to defend the practice.

Extract

Chapter 1 — the cocaine threshold:

Yet upon that afternoon, whether it was the Beaune which I had taken

with my lunch, or the additional exasperation produced by the extreme

deliberation of his manner, I suddenly felt that I could hold out no

longer.

“Which is it to-day?” I asked,—“morphine or cocaine?”

He raised his eyes languidly from the old black-letter volume which he

had opened. “It is cocaine,” he said,—“a seven-per-cent. solution.

Would you care to try it?”

“No, indeed,” I answered, brusquely. “My constitution has not got over

the Afghan campaign yet. I cannot afford to throw any extra strain upon

it.”

He smiled at my vehemence. “Perhaps you are right, Watson,” he said. “I

suppose that its influence is physically a bad one. I find it, however,

so transcendently stimulating and clarifying to the mind that its

secondary action is a matter of small moment.”

“But consider!” I said, earnestly. “Count the cost! Your brain may, as

you say, be roused and excited, but it is a pathological and morbid

process, which involves increased tissue-change and may at last leave a

permanent weakness. You know, too, what a black reaction comes upon

you. Surely the game is hardly worth the candle. Why should you, for a

mere passing pleasure, risk the loss of those great powers with which

you have been endowed? Remember that I speak not only as one comrade to

another, but as a medical man to one for whose constitution he is to

some extent answerable.”

He did not seem offended. On the contrary, he put his finger-tips

together and leaned his elbows on the arms of his chair, like one who

has a relish for conversation.

“My mind,” he said, “rebels at stagnation. Give me problems, give me

work, give me the most abstruse cryptogram or the most intricate

analysis, and I am in my own proper atmosphere. I can dispense then

with artificial stimulants. But I abhor the dull routine of existence.

I crave for mental exaltation. That is why I have chosen my own

particular profession,—or rather created it, for I am the only one in

the world.”

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle presents the character of Sherlock Holmes in The Sign of the Four.

Write about:

- how Conan Doyle presents Holmes in this extract

- how Conan Doyle presents Holmes in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Conan Doyle presents Holmes as the late-Victorian rational genius whose method is also his prison — the deductive faculty that can solve any case but cannot bear ordinary life between cases. The plan tracks Holmes across three levels: stagnation (Chapter 1, cocaine without case), animation (Chapter 5–6, deduction in action at Pondicherry Lodge), and re-stagnation (Chapter 12, the case closed and Holmes returning to the needle).

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Deductive method = reasoning from observed details to a hidden conclusion (modelled on Joseph Bell's Edinburgh diagnostic showmanship).

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Watson as foil to Holmes — domestic to extraordinary; Athelney Jones as foil — institutional to private genius).

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Anaphora = repetition at the start of clauses.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Hook (historical fact): Conan Doyle trained as a medical student at Edinburgh University from 1876 to 1881 under Dr Joseph Bell, who could deduce a patient's occupation and recent travel from their hands, gait and clothing alone — and whom Conan Doyle later named, in his 1924 autobiography, as the direct model for Holmes.

• Building sentence (historical context): The Bell method sat inside a wider late-Victorian moment in which criminal investigation was being turned from amateur questioning into a trained scientific profession. Alphonse Bertillon's anthropometric system of identification (Paris, 1879), the Metropolitan Police CID (founded 1878) and the new sciences of forensic chemistry, ballistics and bloodstain analysis had begun to make the era's faith that systematic observation could replace suspects' confessions into public policy.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's "seven-per-cent. solution" installing Holmes's genius as a cost, the Chapter 5–6 Pondicherry Lodge investigation where the deductive method is applied at full extension, and Chapter 12's circular return where the case closes and Holmes turns back to the needle, Conan Doyle presents Holmes as the late-Victorian rational genius whose method is also his prison — three levels of the same condition, the Bell-inherited deduction sustaining only its own moments of crisis.

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the cocaine threshold — genius installed as cost)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Conan Doyle installs Holmes at his first level — genius as cost, the deductive method's daily price visible in the "seven-per-cent. solution" before any case has even arrived.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "My mind, he said, rebels at stagnation. Give me problems, give me work, give me the most abstruse cryptogram or the most intricate analysis, and I am in my own proper atmosphere" combines anaphora (the imperative "Give me… Give me… Give me…" opening three successive clauses) with the climbing tricolon ("problems, work, cryptogram or intricate analysis" escalating in cognitive demand).

• Close analysis: The verb "rebels" names the mind's relation to inaction as physical resistance; the imperative "Give me" puts the demand for cognitive material in Holmes's own mouth, shifting the cost of his genius onto whoever fails to supply the next problem.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears Holmes's anaphoric demand and registers genius as a need rather than a possession.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader infers from Watson's medical concern ("Count the cost!") that the very deductive method the novel will admire is being installed at scene one as a pathology.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be using the cocaine-threshold to set the cost of genius before the genius has even been demonstrated, so the case the novel is about to deliver feels less like Holmes's triumph than like his temporary relief.

• Context (AO3): Cocaine was sold legally over British counters until 1916; the "seven-per-cent. solution" sits inside the late-Victorian medical-pharmaceutical culture that had already begun to recognise dependency as a clinical condition (Levinstein's Morbid Craving for Morphia, 1878), and Conan Doyle, a trained doctor, installs the diagnosis in the protagonist before the reader has met him on the case.

Scene focus: (Chapter 5–6, Pondicherry Lodge + Athelney Jones — genius applied)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed Holmes's genius as cost, the Chapters 5–6 Pondicherry Lodge investigation forces it into application — the deductive method exercised at full extension against a body, a room, and an institutional rival.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Holmes's exchange with Athelney Jones — "You are an honour to the police service. The principles of your science are very fine, no doubt" — combines dramatic irony (Holmes's apparent compliment masking professional contempt, audible only to Watson and the reader) with the formal vocabulary of "principles" and "science" (Holmes claiming the institutional vocabulary against the institution's own representative).

• Close analysis: The honorific "an honour" sits inside an ironic frame audible to the educated reader but inaudible to Jones; the noun "science" deployed against the bumbling official-detective relocates scientific authority from Scotland Yard to Baker Street.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader catches the dramatic irony and feels the foil-relation between private genius and official bureaucracy land on Jones's bewilderment.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises Holmes's deductive method, in this central scene, as not eccentricity but professional rivalry — the private-detective genre staking its claim against the institutional CID.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing Holmes's genius into a foil-relation with Athelney Jones to dramatise the era's argument over which kind of rationality should be trusted with criminal investigation — Bertillon's institutional system or Bell's individual deduction.

• Context (AO3): The CID had been established at Scotland Yard in 1878 (twelve years before publication); Athelney Jones is the era's institutional detective made into character, his bumbling foil-position confirming for the reader the genre's preference for the private genius.

Scene focus: (Chapter 12, the circular return — genius without object)

• Topic: Where Chapters 5–6 had brought Holmes's genius into application, the Chapter-12 closing scene returns it to the needle — the case finished, the deduction unemployed, the genius once again without object.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "For me, said Sherlock Holmes, there still remains the cocaine-bottle" combines the antithetical structure of the closing (Watson gains a wife; Mary gains nothing; Jones gains the credit; Holmes gains the bottle) with the demonstrative noun phrase "the cocaine-bottle" (the article "the" making the bottle a fixed item already known, waiting on the table since Chapter 1).

• Close analysis: The contrastive "For me" sets Holmes apart from the other endings the novel has just delivered; the definite article in "the cocaine-bottle" signals continuation rather than relapse — the bottle is what was always waiting between cases.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the closing antithesis and recognises the case as merely the interval between two doses.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader infers Holmes's circular return as the genre's structural confession — the detective story exists to give genius a temporary object, but the genius itself remains permanently unsatisfied.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the cost-of-genius diagnosis as the detective genre's hidden truth — the rational-genius hero is also the rational-genius patient, and the case is the only treatment the genre admits.

• Context (AO3): Conan Doyle would publish "The Final Problem" (December 1893) less than four years after The Sign of the Four, killing Holmes off at the Reichenbach Falls in part because of his author's exhaustion with the character's dependency-structure; the Chapter-12 return to the cocaine-bottle is the structural fact Conan Doyle himself would later try to escape.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as the cost of genius in the "seven-per-cent. solution", Chapters 5–6 had forced into application against Pondicherry Lodge and Athelney Jones, and Chapter 12 had returned to the needle once the case was closed — three levels of the same condition, the Bell-inherited deductive method sustaining only its own moments of crisis.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British detective is the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away — and that the rational-genius hero is also the rational-genius patient.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the era's idealised rational genius as a figure whose method is also his prison, the case the genre's only treatment for the genius the genre keeps producing.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the deductive method is the price of itself, that the case is the interval between two doses, and that the closing image of "the cocaine-bottle" is the detective genre's hidden confession — that genius without an object is the cost the era's faith in reason has not learned to admit.

Q2 — The character of Watson

Preamble

In this extract, Miss Mary Morstan arrives at Baker Street and Watson, the narrator, registers his first impression of her.

Extract

Chapter 2 — Miss Morstan's arrival:

Miss Morstan entered the room with a firm step and an outward composure

of manner. She was a blonde young lady, small, dainty, well gloved, and

dressed in the most perfect taste. There was, however, a plainness and

simplicity about her costume which bore with it a suggestion of limited

means. The dress was a sombre greyish beige, untrimmed and unbraided,

and she wore a small turban of the same dull hue, relieved only by a

suspicion of white feather in the side. Her face had neither regularity

of feature nor beauty of complexion, but her expression was sweet and

amiable, and her large blue eyes were singularly spiritual and

sympathetic. In an experience of women which extends over many nations

and three separate continents, I have never looked upon a face which

gave a clearer promise of a refined and sensitive nature. I could not

but observe that as she took the seat which Sherlock Holmes placed for

her, her lip trembled, her hand quivered, and she showed every sign of

intense inward agitation.

“I have come to you, Mr. Holmes,” she said, “because you once enabled

my employer, Mrs. Cecil Forrester, to unravel a little domestic

complication. She was much impressed by your kindness and skill.”

“Mrs. Cecil Forrester,” he repeated thoughtfully. “I believe that I was

of some slight service to her. The case, however, as I remember it, was

a very simple one.”

“She did not think so. But at least you cannot say the same of mine. I

can hardly imagine anything more strange, more utterly inexplicable,

than the situation in which I find myself.”

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle presents the character of Watson in The Sign of the Four.

Write about:

- how Conan Doyle presents Watson in this extract

- how Conan Doyle presents Watson in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Conan Doyle presents Watson at three levels — the wounded Afghan veteran (Chapter 1) whose injury brings the empire home, the chivalric narrator (Chapter 2 onward) whose first-person voice anchors the reader's emotional engagement with the case, and the bourgeois husband (Chapter 12) whose marriage to Mary Morstan resolves the romance plot the detective plot has been carrying inside it.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Watson as foil to Holmes).

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• First-person retrospective narration = the narrator looking back on completed events from a later position.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Pathetic fallacy = the natural world reflecting human emotion.

• Hook (historical fact): The Battle of Maiwand on 27 July 1880, in the Second Anglo-Afghan War, ended in a British defeat that killed over a thousand British and Indian troops — the "Afghan campaign" Watson invokes in the opening pages of The Sign of the Four.

• Building sentence (historical context): Watson's introduction in A Study in Scarlet (1887) had named the "Jezail bullet" he had taken at Maiwand, fixing him biographically to a battle whose memory was still publicly raw a decade later. The empire's wound brought home by an army surgeon supplies the novel's narrator with a body the rest of the case will continue to register through, and with a vocabulary of "black reaction" and "campaign" the case will keep using.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's wounded Afghan veteran whose body still feels the empire's reach exceeding its grasp, Chapter 2's chivalric first-person narration of Mary Morstan's arrival, and Chapter 12's bourgeois resolution into marriage, Conan Doyle presents Watson at three levels — the empire's wounded body, the case's emotional centre, and the romance plot's domestic completion — three stages of the same first-person voice the novel asks the reader to inhabit.

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the wounded Afghan veteran — body installed)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Conan Doyle installs Watson at his first level — the wounded Afghan veteran whose body fixes the empire to the narrator's voice from the novel's first scene.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Watson's response to Holmes's offer of cocaine — "My constitution has not got over the Afghan campaign yet. I cannot afford to throw any extra strain upon it" — combines the medical-clinical noun "constitution" (Watson speaking as physician about his own body) with the financial verb "afford" (the body imagined as a budget).

• Close analysis: The noun "campaign" keeps Watson's body fixed to a specific named war the contemporary reader recognised; the verb "afford" turns physical depletion into an economy in which every further strain has a price.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader feels Watson's wound register inside a medical-financial vocabulary the contemporary period had begun to use about "nervous depletion".

• Effect on reader 2: The reader sees Watson refuse Holmes's cocaine and recognises the foil-relation already operating — Watson's body sets the limit Holmes's mind has not yet accepted.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the empire's wound in the novel's first scene as the silent condition of the narrator's voice — every observation the reader will receive comes filtered through a body the empire has already injured.

• Context (AO3): The Maiwand defeat had been a national trauma three years before A Study in Scarlet introduced Watson; by the time The Sign of the Four appeared in 1890, Watson's "Jezail bullet" had already become public shorthand for the empire's reach exceeding its grasp.

Scene focus: (Chapter 2, Mary Morstan's arrival — chivalric narrator)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed Watson as the empire's wounded body, the Chapter-2 arrival of Mary Morstan converts him into the case's emotional centre — the chivalric narrator whose first-person voice asks the reader to register the case as more than a problem in deduction.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "She was a blonde young lady, small, dainty, well gloved, and dressed in the most perfect taste" combines the asyndetic listing of approving adjectives (four positive qualifiers piled without conjunctions) with the social-class verb of "taste" (Mary's appearance located inside the era's middle-class vocabulary of decorum).

• Close analysis: The compound "well gloved" fixes Mary's gloves as the visible sign of bourgeois respectability; the climactic phrase "perfect taste" lets Watson's narrator-voice slip from observation into approval the case-narrative does not strictly require.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the narrator's affection in the catalogue of approving adjectives and recognises the case as a narrative inside which the narrator has been emotionally invested before the deduction begins.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader sees the chivalric register operate (the narrator's evident gallantry toward Mary's "firm step" and "outward composure") and recognises Watson as the era's reader-surrogate — the gentleman whose decorum the reader has been trained to recognise as correct.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be using Watson's chivalric narration to give the case an emotional content the deductive method itself does not deliver — the reader follows Holmes's reasoning through Watson's feeling.

• Context (AO3): The Married Women's Property Act 1882 had granted middle-class women the legal right to own and control property in their own names for the first time; Mary's "firm step and outward composure" arrives in a literary moment in which the bourgeois Victorian woman was beginning to appear as a person rather than a domestic object — and Watson's chivalric narration registers that arrival.

Scene focus: (Chapter 12, the bourgeois resolution — narrator becomes husband)

• Topic: Where Chapter 2 had converted Watson into the case's emotional centre, the Chapter-12 closing returns him to bourgeois resolution — the narrator becomes husband, the romance plot completed, and the first-person voice handed over to the wife Watson could only afford to marry once the empire's plundered treasure had disappeared into the Thames.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Watson's recognition at the empty treasure-box — "the golden barrier was gone from between us" — combines the metaphor of the golden barrier (the wealth standing physically between Watson and Mary) with the passive past tense "was gone" (the obstacle removed by an action whose agent is the empire-criminal Small, not Watson himself).

• Close analysis: The adjective "golden" makes the obstacle the wealth itself — not class, not war, not professional standing; the passive "was gone" lets the romance-plot completion arrive from outside the romance — Small's act of dispossession enables Watson's act of proposal.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the romance plot resolved by the detective plot's failure to recover the treasure and registers the irony — the empire's plundered wealth had to be lost for the bourgeois marriage to be possible.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader sees Watson leave Holmes's flat to marry Mary and recognises the closing as the genre's hidden contract — the narrator must be returnable to ordinary life so the reader can return with him.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the bourgeois closing as the genre's reassurance — the case's reader-surrogate exits into marriage, leaving the rational-genius hero alone with the cocaine-bottle.

• Context (AO3): The 1882 Married Women's Property Act and the subsequent expansion of married women's legal personhood through the 1880s meant Mary Morstan, after marriage, would retain her own property rights; the romance plot's resolution sits inside that era's reshaped marriage law, which had economically made the bourgeois domestic ending more legally substantial than it would have been a decade earlier.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as the empire's wounded body, Chapter 2 had converted into the chivalric narrator at Mary's arrival, and Chapter 12 had completed in the bourgeois marriage — three stages of the same first-person voice, the empire's wounded surgeon walking the reader from injury through observation into ordinary domestic resolution.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British detective is the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away — and Watson is the bourgeois reader whose injury, observation and marriage together mark the journey the genre asks the reader to take.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing Watson as the genre's reader-surrogate, the wounded gentleman whose first-person voice keeps the case emotionally human even as Holmes's deduction keeps it rational.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the case is what happens between Watson's wound and Watson's wedding, that the empire's injury supplies the narrator's voice, and that the romance plot's resolution requires the empire's plundered treasure to disappear before the bourgeois marriage can be proposed.

Q3 — Mystery and suspense

Preamble

In this extract, Watson stoops to the locked-room hole and recoils at the sight of Bartholomew Sholto's body in the moonlight.

Extract

Chapter 5 — Bartholomew's body in moonlight:

I stooped to the hole, and recoiled in horror. Moonlight was streaming

into the room, and it was bright with a vague and shifty radiance.

Looking straight at me, and suspended, as it were, in the air, for all

beneath was in shadow, there hung a face,—the very face of our

companion Thaddeus. There was the same high, shining head, the same

circular bristle of red hair, the same bloodless countenance. The

features were set, however, in a horrible smile, a fixed and unnatural

grin, which in that still and moonlit room was more jarring to the

nerves than any scowl or contortion. So like was the face to that of

our little friend that I looked round at him to make sure that he was

indeed with us. Then I recalled to mind that he had mentioned to us

that his brother and he were twins.

“This is terrible!” I said to Holmes. “What is to be done?”

“The door must come down,” he answered, and, springing against it, he

put all his weight upon the lock. It creaked and groaned, but did not

yield. Together we flung ourselves upon it once more, and this time it

gave way with a sudden snap, and we found ourselves within Bartholomew

Sholto’s chamber.

It appeared to have been fitted up as a chemical laboratory. A double

line of glass-stoppered bottles was drawn up upon the wall opposite the

door, and the table was littered over with Bunsen burners, test-tubes,

and retorts. In the corners stood carboys of acid in wicker baskets.

One of these appeared to leak or to have been broken, for a stream of

dark-coloured liquid had trickled out from it, and the air was heavy

with a peculiarly pungent, tar-like odour. A set of steps stood at one

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle creates mystery and suspense in The Sign of the Four.

Write about:

- how Conan Doyle creates mystery and suspense in this extract

- how Conan Doyle creates mystery and suspense in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Conan Doyle creates mystery and suspense at three levels — the cryptic setup (Chapter 1's old black-letter volume; Mary's pearl-by-post; the unsigned plan), the Gothic climax (Chapter 5's locked-room horror under moonlight), and the rational resolution (Chapters 11–12's empty treasure-box and Small's confession). The plan tracks the genre's three-act mystery shape from cryptic to Gothic to rational.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion (the "streaming" moonlight at Pondicherry Lodge).

• Apophasis (negation) = describing something by what it is not or by witnesses' inability to name it.

• Frame narrative = a story told inside another story's outer container.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Foreshadowing = early hints of later events.

• Hook (historical fact): Edgar Allan Poe's "The Murders in the Rue Morgue", published in Graham's Magazine in April 1841, founded the modern detective tale by establishing its three rules — a brilliant rational detective (Auguste Dupin), a slower narrator-companion, and a locked-room puzzle.

• Building sentence (historical context): Wilkie Collins's The Moonstone (1868) had then naturalised the form for an English readership, and A Study in Scarlet (1887) had introduced Holmes inside the same convention. The Sign of the Four (Lippincott's, February 1890) is the second Holmes novel, and Conan Doyle constructs its mystery deliberately inside the Poe-inherited shape.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's cryptic setup of unsigned plans and posted pearls, Chapter 5's Gothic locked-room climax under streaming moonlight, and Chapters 11–12's rational resolution in the empty treasure-box and Small's confession, Conan Doyle creates mystery and suspense at three levels — three acts of the same Poe-inherited form, the detective tale operating cryptic, then Gothic, then rational across the novel's twelve chapters.

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the cryptic setup — mystery installed)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Conan Doyle installs mystery and suspense at their first level — cryptically, through the unsigned plan, the "old black-letter volume", and Mary Morstan's pearl-by-annual-post.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Holmes's claim — "I crave for mental exaltation" — followed by Mary's narrative of the "large pearl" arriving each year "in a small cardboard box" combines the abstract noun of "exaltation" (the genre's appetite named) with the unsettling specificity of the "cardboard box" (the cryptic object presented in domestic packaging).

• Close analysis: The verb "crave" assigns the suspense-economy to Holmes's psychology before the case has even appeared; the modest noun phrase "cardboard box" domesticates the cryptic gift to deepen its strangeness.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears Holmes's appetite and recognises that the genre's hunger has been installed before the genre has begun delivering.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader picks up the cardboard-box detail and infers that the cryptic objects of the case have been arriving for years inside the most ordinary domestic vocabulary.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the cryptic setup as the genre's first act — the reader, like Holmes, is asked to read ordinary detail (a box, a pearl, a posting) for the encrypted meaning the rest of the novel will unfold.

• Context (AO3): Wilkie Collins's The Moonstone (1868) had used the same domestic-packaging-of-cryptic-objects strategy in its diamond-stealing plot; Conan Doyle inherits that English version of Poe's American-Gothic device and refits it for the late-Victorian short novel.

Scene focus: (Chapter 5, Bartholomew's body in moonlight — Gothic climax)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed mystery at the level of cryptic setup, the Chapter-5 locked-room scene escalates it to Gothic climax — Watson stooping to the hole to see Bartholomew's body bathed in "streaming" moonlight, the genre's middle act delivered as visible horror.

Frame

Conan Doyle stages science and reason at three levels — the parlour-trick (Chapter 1's watch deduction installs the method as showmanship), the police rivalry (Chapter 5–6 brings the method into competition with the institutional CID), and the colonial-explainer (Chapters 11–12 forces the method to its limit when the case's content turns out to be the empire). Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Athelney Jones as institutional foil to Holmes; Tonga as colonial foil to the deductive method).

• Anaphora = repetition at the start of clauses.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Frame narrative = story told inside another story's outer container.

• Hook (historical fact): Alphonse Bertillon's anthropométrie — the system of identifying repeat criminals through eleven body measurements — was introduced at the Paris Prefecture of Police in 1879, eleven years before The Sign of the Four, and was the world's first institutionalised forensic-identification system.

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the watch deduction — method installed as parlour-trick)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Conan Doyle installs the deductive method at its first level — as parlour-trick, Holmes reading Watson's pocket-watch back to him as if reading a confession from the metal.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "It is difficult for a man to have any object in daily use without leaving the impress of his individuality upon it" combines the abstract noun "impress" (each object recording its owner) with the legal-philosophical noun "individuality" (the era's emerging vocabulary for measurable selfhood).

• Close analysis: The verb "leaving" presents the impress as inevitable, not deliberate — the deductive method's first claim is that the body cannot help recording itself; the noun "individuality" aligns Holmes's deduction with Bertillon's anthropometric premise.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the watch deduced and registers the deductive method as a parlour-trick whose stakes the genre will quickly raise.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader picks up the "impress of his individuality" phrase and recognises it as the era's forensic philosophy translated into Conan Doyle's drawing-room.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the method's first principle inside an apparently casual moment so the reader internalises the philosophy before the case requires it.

• Context (AO3): Bertillon's anthropométrie (Paris, 1879) had argued that the body's measurable details could replace identification by name; Holmes's claim about the "impress of his individuality" on objects extends Bertillon's premise from body to property — the era's forensic philosophy refitted for the private detective's drawing-room.

Scene focus: (Chapter 5–6, Pondicherry Lodge + Athelney Jones — method as institutional rivalry)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed the method as parlour-trick, Chapters 5–6 force it into institutional rivalry — Holmes's deduction set against the CID's bumbling official-detective.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Holmes's deduction at Pondicherry Lodge — "a wooden-legged man… a barefooted accomplice" — combines the climbing tricolon of detail (foot-prints, broken ladder, chemical scent) with the foil-relation between Holmes's silent reading and Jones's "of course I do!" loud certainty.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader feels Holmes's silent deductive sequence and contrasts it with Jones's noisy official certainty.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises the foil-position as the genre's institutional argument — the private genius outperforming the salaried CID detective.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be using the Athelney Jones foil to dramatise the era's argument over which kind of rationality should be trusted with criminal investigation.

• Context (AO3): Scotland Yard's CID, established 1878, was the institutional rationalism the novel sets against Holmes's private deduction; Athelney Jones is the era's institutional detective made into character, his foil-position confirming for the reader the genre's preference for the private genius.

Scene focus: (Chapters 11–12, Small's confession — method's limit at colonial frontier)

• Topic: Where Chapters 5–6 had brought the method into institutional rivalry, Chapters 11–12 force it to its limit — Small's confession arriving as the long second narrative recessed inside the first, supplying the empire-content the deductive method had been circling without naming.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Small's framing — "Now I'll tell you all, every word of it" — followed by his account of the Andaman penal-settlement, the four Sikhs, and the Agra treasure combines the imperative "every word" (the criminal promising to deliver what the detective could not deduce) with the frame narrative (Small's voice recessed inside Watson's, the case's deepest content arriving from a position the rational method itself could not occupy).

• Close analysis: The imperative "every word" surrenders narrative authority to the criminal; the frame structure reveals the rational method requiring confession at the moment its observation-of-objects approach reaches its limit.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader absorbs the criminal's voice take over the narration and recognises the deductive method's reach as ending where the empire's history begins.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader registers the structural concession — the rational method had inferred the case's physical shape (the wooden leg, the small accomplice, the boat) but had not deduced the empire from outside it.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the deductive method's limit honestly — Holmes can read a watch but cannot read the empire; only the empire's broken instrument can.

• Context (AO3): Small's testimony of the Andamans (the British Indian penal colony established 1858 after the Mutiny) names a colonial geography the era's general reader had read about in newspaper-and-administration vocabulary but had rarely heard from the inside; the deductive method's limit is the era's general limit at the imperial frontier.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as parlour-trick in the watch deduction, Chapters 5–6 had brought into institutional rivalry against Athelney Jones, and Chapters 11–12 had forced to its limit at Small's colonial confession — three stages of the same Bell-inherited deductive method, the era's faith in rational observation extended from drawing-room to crime-scene to empire and meeting, at the empire, the limit of what observation alone can deduce.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British detective is the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away — and the deductive method's limit at the colonial confession is the controlling concept's quiet self-correction.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the deductive method as both the era's idealised tool and its honestly admitted limit, the genre's optimism quietly tempered by the form's recognition that the empire requires confession, not deduction.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that science and reason can read the body's "impress of individuality" on a pocket-watch but cannot read the empire's history off the body it has injured — and the case's rational resolution finally arrives only because the criminal speaks.

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle presents ideas about justice and the law in The Sign of the Four.

Write about:

- how Conan Doyle presents justice and the law in this extract

- how Conan Doyle presents justice and the law in the novel as a whole.

Frame

Conan Doyle stages justice and the law at three levels — Holmes's vocational claim (Chapter 1, "the only one in the world"), the institutional foil (Chapter 6, Athelney Jones's bumbling official law), and the extra-legal completion (Chapter 12, Small punished but the treasure destroyed in the Thames). The plan tracks justice across that arc from private vocation through institutional rivalry to the case's completion outside the official record.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Athelney Jones as institutional foil to Holmes).

• Dramatic irony = the audience knows what a character does not.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Frame narrative = story told inside another story's outer container.

• Hook (historical fact): Scotland Yard's Criminal Investigation Department (CID) was established by the Metropolitan Police in 1878, twelve years before The Sign of the Four appeared, formalising plain-clothes detection as a paid public profession for the first time in British history.

• Building sentence (historical context): That institutional moment sat alongside the rising public reputation of the private consulting detective — Holmes's first appearance in A Study in Scarlet (1887) had introduced the alternative to the CID three years before. The era's argument over which form of rational law should be trusted with criminal investigation — institutional bureaucracy or private vocation — supplied Conan Doyle's central foil-relation.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's vocational claim that Holmes is "the only one in the world", Chapter 6's institutional foil between Holmes's silent deduction and Athelney Jones's "of course I do!" certainty, and Chapter 12's extra-legal completion where Small is punished but the Agra treasure is destroyed in the Thames, Conan Doyle stages justice and the law at three levels — three stages of the same argument over what form of justice the late-Victorian period was prepared to call legitimate.

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the vocational claim — private justice installed)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Conan Doyle installs justice and the law at their first level — Holmes's vocational claim, the private detective installing himself as a category the institutional law has not yet caught up with.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Holmes's claim — "That is why I have chosen my own particular profession,—or rather created it, for I am the only one in the world" — combines the self-correcting parenthetical ("or rather created it") with the absolute superlative ("the only one in the world").

• Close analysis: The self-correction "or rather created it" turns vocation into invention — Holmes is not entering a profession but generating one; the absolute "only one" claims the entire category for himself.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader picks up Holmes's vocational claim and recognises the private detective installed as a category the institutional law had not yet recognised.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader catches the self-correction and infers that Holmes's relation to official law is not subordination but parallel jurisdiction.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the private-detective vocation in the novel's first scene so that the institutional foil at Chapter 6 lands inside an argument the novel has already framed.

• Context (AO3): The CID had been founded in 1878 and was still establishing its public legitimacy by 1890; Holmes's vocational claim sits in direct competition with the new institutional category, and the novel will dramatise that competition through Athelney Jones.

Scene focus: (Chapter 6, Athelney Jones — official law refused)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed the private vocation, the Chapter-6 extract dramatises the institutional foil — Holmes's dramatic-ironic compliments to Jones masking professional contempt for the CID's bumbling representative.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Holmes's exchange with Jones — "You are an honour to the police service. The principles of your science are very fine, no doubt" — combines dramatic irony (Holmes's apparent compliment audible only to Watson and the reader) with the formal vocabulary of "principles" and "science" (Holmes claiming the institutional vocabulary against the institution's own representative).

• Close analysis: The honorific "an honour" sits inside an ironic frame audible to the educated reader but inaudible to Jones; the noun "science" relocates scientific authority from Scotland Yard to Baker Street.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader catches the dramatic irony and feels the foil-relation between private genius and official bureaucracy land on Jones's bewilderment.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises Holmes's vocational claim from Chapter 1 now operating against the CID in real-time competition for jurisdiction.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the foil-relation as the genre's quiet argument against the new institutional detective and in favour of the private vocation.

• Context (AO3): The CID had been established at Scotland Yard in 1878; Athelney Jones is the era's institutional detective rendered as bumbling foil, the genre's preference for private deduction over institutional procedure made explicit in scene six.

Scene focus: (Chapter 12, the extra-legal completion — justice outside the official record)

• Topic: Where Chapter 6 had refused official law as bureaucracy, Chapter 12 completes justice extra-legally — Small punished but the Agra treasure destroyed in the Thames, the case's resolution arriving outside the official record the institutional law was supposed to keep.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Small's claim — "I scattered them over a five-mile track" — referring to the treasure flung into the Thames combines the active verb "scattered" (the criminal taking the case's content out of legal jurisdiction by his own hand) with the colonial-imperial register of the "five-mile track" (the geographic specificity that locates the treasure's loss inside London's imperial artery).

• Close analysis: The verb "scattered" makes the criminal the agent of legal-evidentiary destruction; the "five-mile track" gives the loss precise geography, the Thames functioning as both river and imperial conduit.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the case's central evidence destroyed and registers the law's reach as having ended at the Thames.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises that justice is completed not through institutional recovery but through criminal-confessional destruction — the official record left without the case's central object.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the extra-legal completion as the genre's quiet diagnosis — the official law cannot recover what the empire took; only the criminal can dispose of it.

• Context (AO3): The Agra treasure is a fictionalisation of the looted wealth that flowed from the 1857 uprising and the British government's 1858 Government of India Act takeover of the East India Company; its destruction in the Thames is the novel's quiet acknowledgement that the empire's plunder cannot be recovered to legitimate ownership inside the law.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as private vocation in Holmes's "the only one in the world", Chapter 6 had foiled against institutional bureaucracy in Athelney Jones, and Chapter 12 had completed extra-legally in Small's destruction of the Agra treasure — three stages of the same argument, the era's contest between private and institutional justice resolved by a criminal's hand throwing the case's central evidence into the Thames.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British detective is the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away — and that justice, in the empire's wake, is completed by the criminal's confession rather than the law's recovery.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the era's argument over rational law as won by the private detective, but quietly limited by the form's recognition that the imperial-criminal himself disposes of the case's content.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the law's reach ends at the Thames, that the official record cannot hold the empire's loot, and that justice in the late-Victorian detective genre is the private detective's vocational claim completed extra-legally by the criminal's own hand.

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle presents ideas about wealth and greed in The Sign of the Four.

- how Conan Doyle presents wealth and greed in this extract

- how Conan Doyle presents wealth and greed in the novel as a whole.

Frame

Conan Doyle stages wealth and greed at three levels — wealth hoarded (Chapter 4's Sholto apartment installs imperial loot as compulsive private accumulation), wealth killed for (Chapter 5's Bartholomew murdered over the same treasure), and wealth destroyed (Chapter 12's Small flinging the Agra treasure into the Thames). Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Asyndetic listing = list of items piled without conjunctions, producing accumulation.

• Visual / colour imagery = imagery sorted by visual effect.

• Symbolism = an object made to stand for an idea (the treasure as imperial plunder).

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (the Sholto twins as foils to Small — beneficiaries against original plunderer).

• Apostrophe = direct address.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Hook (historical fact): The Indian uprising of 1857 ended with the British government taking direct rule of India from the East India Company under the Government of India Act of 1858 — a transition that legalised vast streams of looted wealth from the Mughal-era treasure-cities of Lucknow, Delhi and Agra into the property of British military officers and officials.

• Building sentence (historical context): By 1890, that flow of imperial wealth had reshaped the British country-house and the suburban villa alike. The Agra treasure of The Sign of the Four is a fictionalised version of the looted Mughal wealth that had passed into British respectable property over the previous three decades, and its trajectory through the novel — hoarded, killed for, and finally scattered in the Thames — dramatises the moral residue of that transition.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 4's Sholto apartment installing the treasure as compulsive private hoarding, Chapter 5's Bartholomew murdered over the same treasure as wealth killed for, and Chapter 12's Small flinging the Agra hoard into the Thames as wealth destroyed, Conan Doyle stages wealth and greed at three levels — three stages of the same imperial loot, its respectability progressively unmade across twelve chapters.

• Topic: In the Chapter-4 Thaddeus apartment scene, Conan Doyle installs wealth and greed at their first level — hoarded compulsively, the imperial loot transformed by its second-generation beneficiary into private aesthetic obsession.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Two great tiger-skins thrown athwart it increased the suggestion of Eastern luxury, as did a huge hookah which stood upon a mat in the corner. A lamp in the fashion of a silver dove was hung from an almost invisible golden wire in the centre of the room" combines an asyndetic listing of orientalist objects (tiger-skins, hookah, silver dove, golden wire) with the colour imagery of "silver" and "golden" (the room flooded with the precious metals the original treasure had supplied).

• Close analysis: The Latinate "athwart" introduces the violence of the tiger-skin's display (the dead animal arranged across the room); the compound "almost invisible" gives the golden wire a presence-in-absence — wealth so embedded in the décor it is no longer registered as wealth.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader sees the room and registers wealth not as accumulated capital but as a compulsive aesthetic — the second-generation beneficiary unable to live without surrounding himself with what his father had taken.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader catches the orientalist register ("Eastern luxury", "hookah") and recognises the loot's continuing colonial shape — the room is the empire decorated.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the imperial-treasure-as-compulsive-décor at scene one of the wealth-and-greed argument, so that the murder over the same treasure (Chapter 5) and its destruction in the Thames (Chapter 12) trace a downward arc the reader has been prepared for.

• Context (AO3): The British country house and suburban villa had been progressively orientalised through the 1860s–80s as imperial loot reshaped middle-class aesthetic taste; Thaddeus's apartment is that wider reshaping condensed into one room.

Scene focus: (Chapter 5, Bartholomew's murder — wealth killed for)

• Topic: Where Chapter 4 had installed wealth as compulsive hoarding, the Chapter-5 Pondicherry Lodge scene escalates it to wealth killed for — Bartholomew's body found at the very moment the treasure is recovered, the imperial loot's ownership disputed by murder.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): The discovery of the body combines pathetic fallacy (the moonlight "streaming" into the locked room as the natural world lighting the murder for the witness) with the visual compression of the death-image — Bartholomew "contorted" in his chair beside the open jewel-box.

• Close analysis: The verb "streaming" gives the moonlight the active motion of revelation; the contorted body beside the jewel-box collapses the question of greed into a single visual — the chair, the body, and the box arranged like a still-life of the era's argument.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader infers the body and the box together and registers the imperial loot as the very thing greed is willing to kill for.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader interprets that the second-generation beneficiary has been overtaken by the original plunderer — Small's claim, framed as repayment of a debt, is also Small's killing-spree.

• Context (AO3): The novel's source-event of the 1857 uprising had itself been a contest over wealth-and-loyalty — the Mughal-era treasure-cities looted by British troops became the basis of fortunes back home; The Sign of the Four relocates that public-imperial contest into the domestic-suburban scale.

Scene focus: (Chapter 12, Small's confession — wealth destroyed)

• Topic: Where Chapter 5 had escalated wealth to murder, Chapter 12 destroys it altogether — Small's confession that he scattered the Agra treasure into the Thames over a five-mile track, the imperial loot finally placed beyond all ownership.

• Close analysis: The compound "five-mile track" renders the destruction as deliberate distribution rather than accidental loss; the verb "scattered" turns Small into the case's agent of disposal — the empire's broken instrument completing the empire's wealth-cycle by destroying it.

• Personification = giving non-human things human qualities.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Apostrophe = direct address.

• Frame narrative = story told inside another story's outer container.

• Hook (historical fact): The Victorian gentleman's club system — the Athenaeum (founded 1824), the Reform (1836), the Garrick (1831), the Travellers (1819) — had institutionalised professional male friendship as the structuring social bond of upper-middle-class life by the 1880s, with London containing more than a hundred clubs by the time Conan Doyle published.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader pities Watson's plea and registers loyalty as the impulse to save the friend from himself.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader shudders at the dual register and recognises the bond as both emotional and professional — the era's gentleman-club model translated into the consulting-room.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing the friendship's central note as care so the later combat (Chapters 9–10) and separation (Chapter 12) land inside a bond the reader has been allowed to invest in early.

• Context (AO3): The era's club culture had institutionalised the professional male-friendship bond; Watson's "comrade" and "medical man" together name the dual register the period had developed — comradely peer plus disciplined professional, the gentleman class's own preferred self-portrait.

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed loyalty as care, Chapters 9–10 escalate it to loyalty as combat — Holmes and Watson firing together from the police launch as the Aurora runs eastward down the Thames.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): The synchronised-fire detail — "our pistols rang out together" — combines the adverb "together" (the friends acting as one body) with the auditory verb "rang out" (the shared discharge becoming a single sound the reader registers).

• Close analysis: The adverb "together" assigns simultaneous agency to Holmes and Watson; the auditory verb "rang" fuses their separate weapons into a single sound, the friendship at maximum combat-coordination.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader registers the synchronised fire and feels the bond at its most active — the friendship in shared action.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader registers the chase scene as the case's physical climax of the Holmes-Watson partnership, the bond's most embodied moment.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the combat scene as the era's idealised version of professional-comrade loyalty — the gentleman class's bond translated into shared physical action under pressure.

• Context (AO3): The Royal Navy's professional culture and the era's volunteer-corps tradition had institutionalised shared-fire male loyalty as a public ideal; Holmes and Watson's synchronised pistol-fire is that ideal translated into the detective-fiction setting.

• Topic: Where Chapters 9–10 had escalated loyalty to combat, Chapter 11's empty treasure-box and Chapter 12's closing convert it to continuance through separation — Watson leaving to marry Mary, Holmes returning to the cocaine-bottle, the bond surviving the divergence.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Watson's reflection at the empty box — "the golden barrier was gone from between us" — combines the metaphor of "the golden barrier" (the wealth physically separating Watson from Mary) with the antithesis between Watson's moving forward (into marriage) and the implicit Holmes remaining behind (with the cocaine-bottle).

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the friendship's continuance through separation as the era's mature model of professional male loyalty — the bond not dependent on shared-roof cohabitation but surviving distinct family and professional trajectories.

• Context (AO3): The era's club-and-professional friendship model had been built on exactly that principle — gentlemen who married, professionalised separately, and yet maintained the bond through shared institutional life; The Sign of the Four's closing pre-figures the future Holmes stories' Watson-revisits-Baker-Street structure.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as loyalty-as-care in Watson's medical-friend plea against the cocaine, Chapters 9–10 had escalated to loyalty-as-combat in the synchronised-fire boat chase, and Chapters 11–12 had converted to loyalty-as-continuance-through-separation at the empty treasure-box — three stages of the same Holmes-Watson bond, professional male friendship operating across care, combat and divergence.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British detective is the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away — and the Holmes-Watson friendship is the bond inside which the reader is asked to follow the case through care, combat and the quiet separation at the close.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the era's idealised professional male friendship as the bond that survives marriage, separation and the divergent trajectories of the cocaine-bottle and the wedding-ring.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that loyalty is not co-residence but care that survives divergence, that combat is the friendship at its most embodied moment, and that the bond's deepest test is the closing where Watson walks out into marriage and the friendship continues, in Watson's first-person narration, on every later case.

In this extract, Watson and Mary Morstan open the Agra treasure-box together at Mrs Forrester's house and discover that it is empty, and Watson immediately proposes.

The box was empty!

No wonder that it was heavy. The iron-work was two-thirds of an inch thick all round. It was massive, well made, and solid, like a chest constructed to carry things of great price, but not one shred or crumb of metal or jewelry lay within it. It was absolutely and completely empty.

“The treasure is lost,” said Miss Morstan, calmly.

As I listened to the words and realised what they meant, a great shadow seemed to pass from my soul. I did not know how this Agra treasure had weighed me down, until now that it was finally removed. Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Conan Doyle presents ideas about love and marriage in The Sign of the Four.

Write about:

- how Conan Doyle presents love and marriage in this extract

- how Conan Doyle presents love and marriage in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Conan Doyle stages love and marriage at three levels — Mary as client (Chapter 2's arrival at Baker Street installs the heroine as composed Victorian woman), Mary embedded in the case (the middle chapters deepen Watson's chivalric attraction through shared investigation), and Mary dispossessed-and-wed (Chapter 11's empty treasure-box turns out to be the romance plot's enabling event). The plan tracks the romance plot's three-stage Victorian shape from arrival through investigation to dispossessed marriage.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Marriage plot = the Victorian fiction-form's central romance trajectory (arrival, courtship, obstacle, resolution).

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Mary as foil to Holmes — domestic to extraordinary).

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Symbolism = an object made to stand for an idea (the empty treasure-box as the romance's enabling sign).

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Hook (historical fact): The Married Women's Property Act of 1882 had granted married women the right to own and control property in their own name for the first time in English law, transforming the Victorian marriage plot's economic terms eight years before The Sign of the Four appeared.

• Building sentence (historical context): That legal change reshaped the Victorian fiction's romance plot — the heroine's economic position was no longer simply absorbed into her husband's at the altar, and the era's novels began to register marriage as a partnership inside which both parties retained legal personhood. Mary Morstan's romance with Watson sits inside that newly reshaped marriage law, and the romance's resolution at the empty treasure-box is the Act's economic logic dramatised in scene.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 2's Mary Morstan arriving at Baker Street as composed client, the middle chapters embedding her inside Watson's chivalric investigation, and Chapter 11's empty treasure-box turning the dispossession into the romance's enabling event, Conan Doyle stages love and marriage at three levels — three stages of the Victorian marriage plot, the heroine progressing from client to investigation-companion to dispossessed-bride across the novel's twelve chapters.

Scene focus: (Chapter 2, Mary's arrival — heroine installed as composed client)

• Topic: In Chapter 2, Conan Doyle installs Mary at her first level — the composed Victorian client, her "firm step and outward composure" introducing the heroine as a person before introducing her as a case.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Miss Morstan entered the room with a firm step and an outward composure of manner. She was a blonde young lady, small, dainty, well gloved, and dressed in the most perfect taste" combines the antithesis between "firm" (active, masculine-coded register) and "composure" (controlled, feminine-coded register) with the asyndetic listing of approving adjectives (four positive qualifiers piled without conjunctions).

• Close analysis: The contrastive pairing of "firm step" and "outward composure" refuses the era's stock distressed-heroine introduction — Mary arrives self-possessed; the listed adjectives place her firmly inside the bourgeois respectable register.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader absorbs Watson's narrator-voice register Mary's composure and recognises the heroine's installation as a person, not an emergency.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader picks up the chivalric register ("perfect taste") and infers Watson's affection has been engaged before the deduction has begun.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be installing Mary at scene-one of the romance plot as a self-possessed Victorian woman, so the case's later chivalric scenes operate inside a relationship the heroine herself can sustain.

• Context (AO3): The Married Women's Property Act of 1882 had given middle-class women the legal right to own and control property; Mary's Chapter-2 self-possession arrives in a literary moment in which the bourgeois Victorian woman was beginning to appear in fiction as a person rather than a domestic object.

Scene focus: (Chapters 4–8, Mary embedded in the investigation — heroine as case-companion)

• Topic: Where Chapter 2 had installed Mary as composed client, the middle chapters embed her inside the investigation — Watson's chivalric attraction deepening through shared case-experience as Mary travels with the detective party from Baker Street to Thaddeus Sholto's apartment to Pondicherry Lodge.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Watson's reflection — "It is of the first importance not to allow your judgment to be biased by personal qualities" (Holmes's caution to Watson about Mary) — combines the legal-formal vocabulary of "judgment" and "bias" (Holmes deploying institutional-rationalist register) with the dramatic-irony of Watson's already-biased narration (the reader hearing Holmes's caution against the very feeling Watson's narrator-voice has been performing for nine chapters).

• Close analysis: The noun "judgment" invokes the legal-rationalist standard the case demands; the noun "bias" names the precise quality Watson cannot prevent — and the dramatic irony lets the reader register Watson's affection as more visible to Holmes than to Watson himself.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader infers Holmes's caution and recognises the romance plot operating inside the detective plot, audible to Holmes before it is audible to Watson.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader interprets the dramatic irony and registers the era's competition between rational-investigative and chivalric-affectionate registers — both legitimate, neither permitted to silence the other.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing Mary's case-embedment as the romance plot's middle act — the heroine no longer separable from the case, her personhood the organising emotional register the deductive method itself does not deliver.

• Context (AO3): The era's emerging vocabulary of romantic engagement (the "engaged couple" as a publicly recognised category, the engagement-ring tradition formalised through the 1860s–80s) had given the romance plot a recognisable middle stage between meeting and proposal; Mary's case-embedment fills that middle stage inside the detective-novel form.

Scene focus: (Chapter 11, the empty treasure-box — heroine dispossessed-and-wed)

• Topic: Where the middle chapters had embedded Mary inside the investigation, Chapter 11's empty treasure-box converts the romance plot's central obstacle into its enabling event — the dispossession that allows the proposal.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Watson's recognition — "a great shadow seemed to pass from my soul" and "the golden barrier was gone from between us" — combines the metaphor of the "golden barrier" (wealth as physical obstacle between Watson and Mary) with the antithesis between Mary's calm response ("The treasure is lost") and Watson's emotional release ("a great shadow seemed to pass from my soul").

• Close analysis: The metaphor "golden barrier" makes the obstacle the wealth itself, not class or war or station; the antithesis between Mary's calm and Watson's release lets the reader register the emotional weight as Watson's, while Mary's composure remains intact.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader understands Watson's relief and recognises the romance plot's central irony — the case the lovers have spent the novel pursuing must fail before the marriage can succeed.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader grasps Mary's "The treasure is lost," spoken "calmly" and infers her self-possession has not been altered by the dispossession — the heroine remains the composed Victorian woman of Chapter 2.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the dispossession-as-enabling-event as the era's revised romance plot — the post-1882 marriage plot resolving not by the heroine's economic absorption into her husband's wealth but by the wealth's removal so neither party need worry about mercenary motive.

• Context (AO3): The Married Women's Property Act 1882 had explicitly addressed the era's anxiety that marriages-for-wealth corrupted both parties; The Sign of the Four's dispossession-and-proposal sequence is one of the period's clearest fictional dramatisations of the Act's economic logic — the wealth gone, the marriage clean.

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 2 had installed as the composed client, the middle chapters had embedded inside the investigation, and Chapter 11 had completed in the empty treasure-box's dispossession-and-proposal — three stages of the Victorian marriage plot, the heroine progressing from client to case-companion to dispossessed-bride across twelve chapters.

• Controlling concept: The novel argues that the British Empire produces the wealth, the violence and the crime that the British detective is then called upon to explain — and the romance plot's resolution requires the empire's stolen Mughal hoard to disappear into the Thames before the bourgeois marriage can be proposed clean.

• Author's purpose: Conan Doyle may be writing the dispossession-as-enabling-event as the post-1882 Victorian romance plot at full extension — the marriage law's economic logic dramatised in scene through one composed Victorian woman and one wounded Afghan veteran.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that love and marriage in The Sign of the Four require the empire's wealth to disappear before the proposal can be honest, that Mary remains self-possessed across all three stages, and that the romance plot's deepest resolution is the empty treasure-box's "golden barrier… gone from between us" — the marriage clean because the loot is in the Thames.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 questions

These three context factors are the period-anchors most useful inside any chapter. Pick one per body paragraph, thread it briefly into the close analysis, and never bolt it on as a stand-alone sentence at the end of the essay.

1. Late-Victorian imperial anxiety + the 1857 Indian uprising + the Andamans penal colony (established 1858) + the Government of India Act 1858. Most useful for: Q2, Q5, Q6, Q7, Q8 — any question on Watson / law / wealth / empire / London. The Battle of Maiwand (27 July 1880) supplies the specific Afghan-war anchor for Watson's wound. The 1858 Andamans penal colony supplies the specific colonial geography of Tonga and Small's prior history. The Agra treasure is a fictionalisation of the looted Mughal-era wealth that flowed from the 1857 events into British respectable property.

2. Late-Victorian detective fiction emergence. Most useful for: Q1, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q9 — any question on Holmes / mystery / science / law / friendship. Edgar Allan Poe's "The Murders in the Rue Morgue" (1841) founded the modern detective tale's three rules. Wilkie Collins's The Moonstone (1868) naturalised the form for English readers. A Study in Scarlet (1887) introduced Holmes; The Sign of the Four (1890) is the second Holmes novel. Joseph Bell's Edinburgh diagnostic showmanship modelled Holmes's deductive method; the CID was established at Scotland Yard in 1878.

3. Late-Victorian forensic science + the 1882 Married Women's Property Act + Charles Booth's London poverty surveys. Most useful for: Q4, Q8, Q10 — any question on science / London / love and marriage. Bertillon's anthropométrie (Paris, 1879) supplied the era's first institutional forensic-identification system; Galton's Finger Prints would arrive in 1892, two years after publication. The Married Women's Property Act 1882 reshaped the era's marriage-plot economics. Booth's London poverty surveys (begun 1886; first map 1889) had publicly mapped the West / East End moral split the cab journey crosses.

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 questions

Pick one critical voice per essay. Name the critic + their work + their year. Embed the critical voice inside a body-paragraph close, NOT as a stand-alone sentence in the conclusion.

1. Patrick Brantlinger, Rule of Darkness: British Literature and Imperialism, 1830–1914 (Cornell University Press, 1988). Brantlinger names the late-Victorian "imperial Gothic" as the form in which the empire returns home as horror, and reads The Sign of the Four as one of the genre's founding texts — the empire's wealth, violence and narrative imported into the domestic detective tale. Apply to: any question about empire / Hyde-like atavistic figures / Tonga / Small / wealth / London — Brantlinger's reading frames the novel as the era's diagnostic mirror of imperial anxiety.

2. Stephen Knight, Form and Ideology in Crime Fiction (Indiana University Press, 1980). Knight reads detective fiction as a social-ideological form, arguing that Holmes embodies the era's bourgeois rationalist ideology — the private-genius detective whose deduction reassures the middle-class reader that the era's contradictions can be reasoned away. Apply to: any question about Holmes / Watson / mystery / science / law / friendship — Knight's reading frames the genre as the era's ideological reassurance and quietly reads its own limits.

Reminder

• One context factor per body paragraph, threaded into the close, never bolted on at the end.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, embedded inside a body-paragraph close.

• The controlling concept's three claims — the empire produces the wealth, the violence and the crime the detective is called upon to explain; Holmes's deductive method dramatises the era's faith that imperial trouble can be reasoned away; the novel's trajectory keeps showing the empire returning home as the case's deepest content — should land at least once across the introduction's thesis and the conclusion's restated thesis.

• The locked structure — Intro 3 / BP1-2-3 each 7 / Conclusion 4 — is non-negotiable. AQA examiners read for shape; the page must look like an essay.

• Whole-essay cohesion — BP2 opens "Where Chapter X had installed Y…", BP3 opens "Where Chapter Z had Y…", conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns.

• Tonga / colonial caricature — read historically, not exotically. Hold the era's racial-ideological vocabulary at the same distance as one holds Holmes's cocaine — both the period's symptoms, neither its credentials.

Total target: ~1000 words on the page in the exam. Always under 1100. Always close on the controlling concept's final landing point — the case's deepest content is the empire, and the deductive method had to admit the criminal's voice to deliver it.
